mission.title: Install and Configure Kyverno (Policy Engine)
mission.description: Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine that allows users to manage security, compliance, and governance through policy-as-code. It is particularly useful for teams looking to enforce policies without needing to learn a new language. Key trade-offs include the need for careful policy design to avoid unintended consequences and potential performance impacts on the cluster.

steps.title: Add the Kyverno Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Kyverno Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno/

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the Kyverno repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install Kyverno
steps.description:
This step installs Kyverno using Helm, specifying the namespace and version.

```bash

kubectl create namespace kyverno

helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version 1.17.1

```

> Creates a namespace for Kyverno.
> Installs Kyverno in the created namespace.

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Kyverno pods are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Lists all pods in the kyverno namespace to verify they are running.

steps.title: Create an example policy
steps.description:
This step creates a sample policy to demonstrate Kyverno's functionality.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: 'Label `app` is required.'
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: '?'
EOF

```

> Creates a policy that requires a specific label on Pods.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: 'Label `app` is required.'
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: '?'
EOF

uninstall.title: Uninstall Kyverno
uninstall.description:
This step removes Kyverno and all associated resources. Be aware that any policies created will also be deleted, which may lead to data loss if those policies were enforcing critical configurations.

```bash

helm uninstall kyverno --namespace kyverno

kubectl delete namespace kyverno

```

> Uninstalls the Kyverno release.
> Deletes the Kyverno namespace.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Kyverno
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your policies. This step will upgrade Kyverno to the latest version while ensuring the cluster remains stable.

```bash

kubectl get clusterpolicies -o yaml > clusterpolicies-backup.yaml

helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version 1.17.1

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Backs up existing cluster policies.
> Upgrades Kyverno to the specified version.
> Verifies that the upgraded pods are running.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'failed to validate resource'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the policy is incorrectly defined or if the resource does not match the policy criteria.

**Fix:**
Review the policy definition and ensure it matches the resource specifications.

troubleshooting.title: Kyverno pods are in 'CrashLoopBackOff'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if there is a misconfiguration in the Kyverno deployment or if the required resources are not available.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Kyverno pods using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kyverno' for more details.

troubleshooting.title: Policies are not being enforced
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may indicate that the policies are not correctly applied or that the Kyverno controller is not running.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the Kyverno controller is running and that the policies are applied correctly.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'unable to recognize' when applying policies
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can occur if the policy uses an unsupported API version.

**Fix:**
Verify that the API versions used in the policy are compatible with your Kubernetes version.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new validation rules and improved error messages.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the use of certain legacy validation patterns.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: Review and update any deprecated patterns in existing policies.

resolution.summary: You should see the Kyverno pods running with a status of 'Running' when executing 'kubectl get pods -n kyverno'.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: 'Label `app` is required.'
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: '?'
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.25 or higher with the necessary tools installed.
